text: The surgeon begins the colectomy by entering the omental bursa inferior to the gastroepiploic vessels after a thorough exploration for metastasis.
The surgeon begins the hemicolectomy by entering the omental bursa inferior to the gastroepiploic vessels, following a careful exploration for metastasis.
The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure of the colon, taking care to avoid the gallbladder during the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure of the colon, taking care to avoid the gallbladder during the procedure.
The surgeon detaches the anterior lobe of the transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreatic neck, exposing the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon detaches the anterior lobe of the transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreatic neck, exposing the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is visible, with gauze left at the SMV as a landmark for the subsequent dissection.

The surgeon leaves a gauze at the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) as a landmark for dissection. The pedicle of the middle colic vessels and ileocolic vessels are grasped to identify the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The peritoneum is divided near the left border of the SMA, extending from the left of the middle colic vessels to the inferior border of the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon grasps the pedicle of the middle colic and ileocolic vessels to identify the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The peritoneum is divided near the left border of the SMA, extending from the left of the middle colic vessels to the inferior border of the ileocolic vessels.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound knife to dissect a thin layer of fatty tissue overlying the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), carefully avoiding injury to vessels within the thick adipose tissue. The entire length of the SMA is revealed, and the sheath of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is divided from medial to lateral and from caudal to cranial.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound knife to dissect a thin layer of fatty tissue overlying the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), carefully avoiding injury to vessels within the thick adipose tissue. The sheath of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is then divided from medial to lateral and from caudal to cranial, revealing the entire length of the SMA.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery and proceeds to ligate the root of the middle colonic artery and vein. Subsequently, the iliocolic vein and artery are identified, and their roots are ligated.

The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery and proceeds to ligate the root of the middle colonic artery and vein. Subsequently, the iliocolic vein is identified, followed by the ligation of the root of the ileocolic vein and artery. The iliocolic artery is also identified during this process.

The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the duodenum, tracing the gastroepiploic trunk (GTH) up to the right gastroepiploic veins. The point where the right colic vein connects with the GTH is identified, allowing for the safe clipping and division of the right colic vein.

The surgeon separates the transverse mesocolon from the duodenum, tracing the gastrocolic trunk of Hennel (GTH) up to the right gastroepiploic veins. The point where the right colic vein connects with the GTH is identified, allowing for safe clipping and division of the right colic vein.
The gastrocolic trunk of Hennel (GTH) is visible. A drainage tube is placed due to the extended lymphadenectomy, with the intention to monitor for potential chyle leaks.
